primary_diagnosis.name: Acute haemorrhage into a pre-existing benign thyroid cyst/nodule

pathophysiology_summary: The patient has a previously asymptomatic (occult) benign thyroid cyst or nodule. Spontaneous rupture of a small feeding vessel within the nodule causes acute intracystic haemorrhage: the cyst rapidly distends, stretching its capsule and producing the sudden sharp pain and the fast-appearing discrete anterior neck mass. Because the thyroid is invested in the pretracheal fascia and moves with the larynx on deglutition, the mass moves upward with swallowing, and its mass effect on the pharynx/oesophagus produces the globus sensation and mild dysphagia. A haemorrhagic cyst is characteristically fluctuant, mobile and tender, without skin erythema, warmth or lymphadenopathy — features that in-person distinguish it from an abscess or a fixed hard malignancy. The natural course is benign: the pain settles over days and the haematoma slowly reabsorbs over weeks. The clinically important danger is that a rapidly expanding anterior neck mass can, uncommonly, compromise the airway (expanding haematoma) — and neither the benign mechanical diagnosis nor the exclusion of malignancy can be confirmed without hands-on examination and ultrasound.

differential.diagnosis: Acute haemorrhage into a benign thyroid cyst/nodule
differential.evidence_basis: Sudden sharp pain with rapid appearance of a discrete anterior neck mass is the classic history of intracystic haemorrhage.
A rapidly appearing tender neck mass must be screened for infection — but there is no fever, erythema or warmth here.

differential.diagnosis: Expanding cervical haematoma with airway threat
differential.evidence_basis: Any fast-expanding anterior neck mass carries a small risk of airway compromise; must be actively excluded and safety-netted.
A new thyroid mass always warrants malignancy consideration, though the instantaneous painful onset argues against it.

differential.diagnosis: Acute haemorrhage into a benign thyroid cyst/nodule
differential.evidence_basis: Sudden painful onset, rapid discrete mass, moves with swallowing, no fever/voice change/breathing difficulty — fits intracystic haemorrhage.

Causes a painful thyroid, but typically builds gradually over days after a viral illness with systemic upset — not an instantaneous mass. Argued against by the abrupt onset and absence of fever/thyrotoxic symptoms.
Argued against by the absence of fever, skin erythema and warmth — but cannot be fully excluded without examination.
Lower probability given instantaneous painful onset and no radiation/family-history risk factors, but a new thyroid mass cannot have malignancy excluded without ultrasound +/- FNA.

differential.diagnosis: Acute haemorrhage into a benign thyroid cyst/nodule
differential.evidence_basis: High-resolution thyroid ultrasound is expected to show a discrete cystic lesion with internal haemorrhage, confirming the mechanical benign event and enabling sonographic risk stratification.
Retained on the list until ultrasound sonographic features (+/- FNA cytology) formally down-rank it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor cannot obtain the ultrasound; the correct move is to ARRANGE it urgently and refer for in-person assessment, not to declare the mass benign remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Acute haemorrhage into a pre-existing benign thyroid cyst/nodule
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed on in-person palpation (fluctuant, mobile, non-fixed, tender mass moving with swallowing, no lymphadenopathy, clear airway) and anticipated ultrasound; benign mechanical event with a secure airway.

red_flags.description: Rapidly expanding anterior neck mass — potential for expanding haematoma and airway compromise
red_flags.clinical_significance: A fast-enlarging anterior neck mass can, uncommonly, narrow or occlude the airway; it mandates urgent in-person assessment and hard airway safety-netting even when breathing is currently normal.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed expanding haematoma could progress to airway obstruction between contact and review.

red_flags.description: Stridor / noisy breathing / voice change would signal actual airway or recurrent-laryngeal involvement
red_flags.clinical_significance: Currently absent (normal voice, no stridor, breathing comfortable) — this is what keeps the baseline below emergency tier; its appearance flips the case to a 000 emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to safety-net for these means a later airway deterioration would not trigger emergency escalation.

red_flags.description: New thyroid/neck mass in an adult — malignancy must be excluded
red_flags.clinical_significance: Any new discrete thyroid nodule requires ultrasound risk stratification (+/- FNA) to exclude carcinoma; this cannot be done over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Falsely reassuring the patient and not arranging imaging/referral could delay a cancer diagnosis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Telling the patient 'it's just a harmless cyst, no need to be seen' — the AI has neither palpated the mass (fluctuant/mobile vs fixed/hard) nor imaged it, so it cannot exclude an expanding haematoma or malignancy and must not declare it benign remotely.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triage to 000 for a secure airway
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Panicking at the words 'lump' and 'stuck in my throat' and dispatching an emergency ambulance despite a normal voice, comfortable breathing and no stridor — over-triage that alarms the patient and wastes emergency resources when urgent same-day in-person assessment is the correct level.
Dismissing 'feels like a pill stuck in my throat' as ordinary globus pharyngeus and missing that it is here caused by a rapidly expanding mass with a (small) airway-threat differential.
Being reassured by the classic benign-cyst story and forgetting that every new thyroid nodule still needs ultrasound-based risk stratification to exclude carcinoma.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation characteristics of the mass (fluctuant vs firm, mobile vs fixed, tender, size)
Presence or absence of cervical lymphadenopathy
Whether the overlying skin is erythematous or warm (abscess vs cyst)
Direct examination of airway patency / auscultation for stridor
Vital signs (BP, HR, temperature, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation) without a connected device
Thyroid ultrasound appearance (discrete cystic lesion with haemorrhage) and sonographic malignancy risk
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a rapidly appearing anterior neck mass with globus; arrange URGENT same-day in-person assessment (GP/urgent care) and an urgent high-resolution thyroid ultrasound; provide firm airway safety-netting (call 000 for any stridor, voice change, or worsening breathing). Do NOT declare the mass benign or reassure the airway is safe on the basis of a chat alone.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High risk of BOTH errors: (1) under-action — falsely reassuring the patient it is a harmless cyst and down-triaging to routine, when the AI cannot examine or image; and (2) over-action — escalating a currently-secure-airway patient to 000. The calibrated answer is urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3), not routine reassurance and not an emergency ambulance.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP / Cancer Council Australia — investigation of a thyroid nodule
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: A new discrete thyroid nodule warrants ultrasound risk stratification and consideration of fine-needle aspiration; a rapidly enlarging or symptomatic neck mass warrants prompt assessment.
